acceptance_evidence.criterion_1: examples/case-study-flask-cli/app/routes.py:26-27 — def echo_payload(msg: str) -> dict: return {"echo": msg}
acceptance_evidence.criterion_2: examples/case-study-flask-cli/app/server.py:42-50 — /echo branch uses urlparse + parse_qs and calls echo_payload(msg)
acceptance_evidence.criterion_3: examples/case-study-flask-cli/tests/test_routes.py:36-45 — test_echo_payload_with_msg / _without_msg / _empty_msg (3 tests present, though see F1)
acceptance_evidence.criterion_4: Independent rerun: `cd examples/case-study-flask-cli && PYTHONPATH=. pytest tests -q` → 7 passed
acceptance_evidence.criterion_5: Existing tests health/index/version/ping all still pass (lines 16-33 unchanged in spirit; rerun confirms)

findings.title: test_echo_payload_without_msg and test_echo_payload_empty_msg are functionally identical
findings.evidence: examples/case-study-flask-cli/tests/test_routes.py:40-45 — both tests call echo_payload("") and assert {"echo": ""}. There is no distinct 'absent msg' case at the helper level (helper only accepts a str), and the server-level fallback that converts absent msg to "" is not exercised by either test.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) make _without_msg drive the server handler (e.g. via an http client / handler unit test) to actually exercise the parse_qs absence branch in server.py:44-48, or (b) replace one of the two duplicate tests with a distinct case such as URL-encoded msg (%20, +, unicode) or repeated ?msg=a&msg=b.

findings.title: Server-layer /echo query parsing is not covered by any test
findings.evidence: examples/case-study-flask-cli/app/server.py:42-50 — the parse_qs/urlparse logic, the 'msg' key check, the values[0] selection, and the empty-list guard all live in DemoHandler.do_GET, but tests/test_routes.py only imports pure helper functions. No test instantiates DemoHandler or exercises HTTP query parsing.
findings.recommendation: Add at least one integration-style test that starts HTTPServer on an ephemeral port (or invokes BaseHTTPRequestHandler directly) and asserts `/echo?msg=hello`, `/echo` (no query), and `/echo?msg=` all return the expected JSON with Content-Type: application/json. This is the only way criterion_2 is observably proven.

findings.title: Repeated msg parameter silently drops later values
findings.evidence: examples/case-study-flask-cli/app/server.py:45-46 — `values = qs["msg"]; msg = values[0] if values else ""`. A request `/echo?msg=a&msg=b` returns {"echo": "a"} and silently discards 'b'.
findings.recommendation: Either document this 'first-wins' choice in a docstring on do_GET or echo_payload, or explicitly reject multi-value msg with HTTP 400. Current behaviour is defensible but undocumented.

findings.title: parse_qs default drops blank values, masking the empty-vs-absent distinction
findings.evidence: examples/case-study-flask-cli/app/server.py:43 — parse_qs(parsed.query) uses default keep_blank_values=False. A request `/echo?msg=` produces an empty qs dict, falls through to the else branch (msg=""), so blank-msg and absent-msg are server-side indistinguishable. Worker's R001 notes claim 'msg present, absent, or empty all yield the correct JSON', but the implementation collapses two of those cases into one path.
findings.recommendation: If the intent is to honour an explicit empty msg as 'present but empty', pass keep_blank_values=True to parse_qs. Otherwise, update the worker notes / a docstring to reflect that empty and absent are deliberately merged.

findings.title: Test naming style differs from existing tests
findings.evidence: examples/case-study-flask-cli/tests/test_routes.py:21 — existing test test_index_payload_mentions_service uses a 'mentions_X' suffix that describes the assertion; new tests at lines 36-45 use 'with/without/empty_msg' which describe input. Minor consistency issue.
findings.recommendation: Optional rename for consistency, e.g. test_echo_payload_returns_msg / test_echo_payload_defaults_empty_string. Not blocking.

notes: All 5 acceptance criteria are met at the letter of the contract: echo_payload exists, server has /echo branch with query parsing, three echo tests are present, pytest is green (7 passed, 0 failed), and no regression to the original 4 tests. Two substantive review concerns (F1, F2) remain: the three new tests cover only the pure helper and two of them are functionally identical, leaving the server-side parse_qs branch (the actual contract surface) untested. F3-F5 are informational. Recommend approving the relay handoff and filing a follow-up task to add a server-layer integration test for /echo.
